sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 50-year-old male, presents with a 3-week history of abdominal pain located on the right-hand side of the abdomen, underneath the ribs, described as a severe squeezing pain that comes and goes, accompanied by nausea and occasional low-grade fevers.
The pain is exacerbated by eating, particularly high-fat foods, and has not improved with dietary modifications.
The patient has a history of type 2 diabetes, managed with diet and exercise, and has lost 25 pounds over the past 3 months.
He reports a family history of gallbladder disease, with both parents having undergone cholecystectomies.
Past medical history: The patient has type 2 diabetes, managed with diet and exercise, and has lost 25 pounds over the past 3 months.
He reports a family history of gallbladder disease, with both parents having undergone cholecystectomies.
Review of systems: The patient reports occasional low-grade fevers, nausea, and vomiting, but denies fever.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure was 128/88, respiratory rate was 16, pulse rate was 68, and oxygen saturation on room air was 99%.
Physical exam: The patient has a soft, nondistended abdomen with positive bowel sounds.
The patient is guarded in the right upper quadrant, with slight guarding and tenderness to palpation, but no rebound tenderness.
Murphy's sign is positive.
Test Results: Abdominal ultrasound shows multiple gallstones present in the gallbladder, mild thickening of the gallbladder wall, and a normal common bile duct size of 4.1 mm.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has mild cholecystitis with gallstones, likely caused by a gallbladder inflammation that has occurred over the past few weeks.
Problem list: Cholecystitis with gallstones; Suspected gallbladder disease; Type 2 diabetes; Family history of gallbladder disease.
Plan:
I recommended a laparoscopic cholecystectomy to remove the gallbladder.
The surgery is expected to take about an hour to an hour and a half, and the patient will likely spend the night in the hospital for monitoring.
The patient will need to avoid high-fat foods and eat high-fiber foods after surgery.
The patient will be able to get up and walk around within a day or two, and will be seen again in about a week for follow-up.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in about a week for follow-up and to be discharged.
The patient will also need to follow up with their primary care provider to manage their type 2 diabetes.
